290.Option: The model assumes that the proportion of individuals with high-intensity infections remains constant over time, which allows for consistent estimation of prevalence changes even with incomplete data
Evidence: Initially, each individual in a specified population is categorized into one of the four CS but may transition to another CS after the first year of intervention: the changes between these four CS are defined as transition probabilities (TP); the 16 TP corresponding to each of the possible changes are captured in a four-by-four TP matrix.

For example, Fig 1 shows that if 37% of heavy-intensity hookworm infections become light-intensity infections after the first year of preventive chemotherapy, the same proportion (37%) of the remaining heavy-intensity infections will become light-intensity infections after the second year of the intervention.
290.Rationale: The model assumes constant transition probabilities between intensity classes, not constant proportions. Proportions can change as individuals transition between classes based on the TP. The passage describes how infections move between zero, light, moderate, and heavy intensity categories, with TP dictating these movements, so the proportion in each class is dynamic.

290.Option: The model assumes that the biological and epidemiological processes driving infection intensity transitions remain stable over time, enabling long-term projections even with incomplete or imprecise data
Evidence: The Markov model assumes the 'stationarity' of the TP, in the sense that if the intervention remains the same (e.g. albendazole is administered once a year) the TPMS will also remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme.

This assumption is based on: the remarkable capacity of STH to contaminate the environment: a soil survey conducted in 2013 in the Philippines in an area with STH prevalence of 44% found STH eggs in 85% of soil samples collected inside houses, in backyards or from the paths around the village [6]. the long survival time of STH eggs in the environment: several weeks for hookworms [7], a few years for T. trichiura [8] and more than 10 years for A. lumbricoides [9].

Additionally, we assume that the pharmacological interventions conducted in only a part of the population (i.e. school-aged children) do not reduce significantly the faecal contamination of the soil in the short term.
290.Rationale: The core assumption of stationarity means that transition probabilities (TP) remain constant over time if the intervention is unchanged. This stability in biological and epidemiological processes, such as environmental contamination and egg survival, allows the model to project long-term trends even with limited data, as used in simplified models SM1 and SM2.

290.Option: The model assumes that the diagnostic accuracy of Kato-Katz improves over time as control programs gain experience, allowing for more reliable long-term trend estimation
Evidence: In all the datasets used in the present study (i.e. those from the studies used to calculate the model parameters and those from control programmes used to evaluate the prediction capacity of the model), Kato-Katz was the only method used to collect parasitological data. We understand the poor precision of the diagnostic methods traditionally used to monitor STH control programmes (i.e. Kato-Katz) especially when intensities of infection are low [1].

We recognize that the prevalence would have been higher if measured with a more precise method than Kato-Katz, but we wanted to provide programme managers with a tool capable of interpreting the imperfect data collected during routine control activities.
290.Rationale: The passage acknowledges the poor precision of Kato-Katz but does not assume any improvement over time. The model is designed to work with imperfect data, and no mention is made of diagnostic accuracy improving with experience. Instead, it accepts the limitations and models based on available data.

290.Option: The model assumes that reinfection rates decline over time due to reduced environmental contamination, which allows for stable long-term projections despite limited follow-up data
Evidence: During the development of the model we observed that when the entire population is treated (as is the case when albendazole is distributed for control of lymphatic filariasis), the predicted prevalence was consistently higher than the observed values; this discrepancy between the two predictions was increasing at the increase of the baseline prevalence. We interpret this discrepancy as a progressive decrease of reinfection due to the reduction of soil contamination consequent to administration of anthelminthics to the entire population.

We adjusted for this factor by including a standard modification, dependent on the initial level of prevalence: if baseline prevalence is > 80% the standard modification becomes active after 5 years of follow up; for lower initial prevalence the standard modification becomes active after 3–4 years of follow up. This modification is only applied when the entire population is treated.
290.Rationale: The assumption of declining reinfection rates is not the core underlying assumption; it is an adjustment made only in specific cases (when the entire population is treated). The primary assumption for long-term projections is stationarity of TP, which implies constant reinfection rates under standard conditions. This option describes an exception, not the general assumption enabling projections with limited data.

290.Option: The model assumes that the effectiveness of anthelminthic drugs increases over time due to cumulative exposure, enabling stable long-term projections despite data limitations
Evidence: The Markov model assumes the 'stationarity' of the TP, in the sense that if the intervention remains the same (e.g. albendazole is administered once a year) the TPMS will also remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme.

For example, Fig 1 shows that if 37% of heavy-intensity hookworm infections become light-intensity infections after the first year of preventive chemotherapy, the same proportion (37%) of the remaining heavy-intensity infections will become light-intensity infections after the second year of the intervention.
290.Rationale: The model assumes transition probabilities, which include drug effects, remain constant over time, not that they increase. There is no mention of cumulative exposure enhancing drug effectiveness. The stationarity assumption implies that the impact of interventions is consistent across time periods if the intervention is unchanged.

291.Option: Including open-ended questions to capture patient-reported symptoms and improve the comprehensiveness of the assessment
Evidence: two open-ended questions were included as a preliminary step to try and capture the most important aspects for the patient
The aim was to assess the patient’s perspectives with a view to adapting the tool or developing a relevant patient reported outcome measure encompassing HRQoL assessment in the future.
291.Rationale: This methodological choice focuses on capturing patient-reported outcomes to improve comprehensiveness, but it does not directly address the standardization of clinical sign interpretation across different evaluators. It is aimed at patient perspectives rather than reducing inter-rater variability in assessing clinical signs.

291.Option: Excluding erythema and pain from assessment because they are not universally present in all LCL patients
Evidence: erythema was not appreciated as a reliable or reproducible sign in skin of colour and pain was not considered as a universal symptom of LCL by the stakeholders
erythema was not considered a reliable measure of treatment efficacy especially in skin of colour
291.Rationale: While this exclusion is based on the lack of reliability and universality of certain signs, it does not directly standardize how the remaining clinical signs are interpreted across evaluators. It reduces the scope of assessment but does not mitigate measurement bias from inconsistent interpretation among raters.

291.Option: Using a nominal group technique to achieve consensus on the inclusion of clinical signs in the outcome measure
Evidence: The NGT approach was performed by having face-to-face discussions at a workshop in March 2018 in Sri Lanka... to reach a final consensual agreement on the set of Core Outcome Domains.
the nominal group technique (NGT) to reach a final consensual agreement on the set of Core Outcome Domains
291.Rationale: The nominal group technique is used to achieve consensus on what clinical signs or domains to include in the outcome measure, but it does not directly address how to standardize the interpretation of those signs across different evaluators. It focuses on content selection rather than measurement consistency.

291.Option: Developing a new palpability score to quantify induration and ulcer edge characteristics
Evidence: a new palpability score was developed to assess the edge of the ulcerated and the overall induration of the non-ulcerated lesions
a newly developed subjective assessment (a palpability score of 0,3,6,9) was described for both non-ulcerated and ulcerated lesions
291.Rationale: Developing a palpability score introduces a quantitative measure for specific signs, but it does not inherently reduce inconsistent interpretation across evaluators. The standardization that mitigates bias comes from the accompanying visual and textual instructions, not merely the creation of the score itself.

291.Option: Incorporating visual and textual standardization in the case report form to reduce inter-rater variability in clinical sign assessment
Evidence: Written as well as diagrammatic and photographic instructions with clinical examples were provided for measuring and assessing LCL to minimize any potential ambiguities.
Schematic images and/or photos alongside descriptions were used to standardise the assessment.
Instructions: for ulcerated lesions measure by palpating the “edge” of the ulcerated lesion.
291.Rationale: This methodological choice directly addresses the risk of measurement bias by providing standardized visual and textual guidelines in the case report form, ensuring that all evaluators interpret clinical signs consistently. This reduces inter-rater variability and enhances the reliability of the outcome measure.

292.Option: Misunderstanding of the Ebola laboratory test process led individuals to delay seeking care until they were certain of their diagnosis
Evidence: WHO reported that between August and December 2014 the time between the onset of Ebola symptoms and hospitalization averaged two to three days [9]. This time lag may be explained by a reluctance to seek care.
In about half of interviews, respondents said they would start by treating themselves with medicines normally used for other illnesses such as malaria, typhoid or cholera.
Several participants claimed to know what the fever would feel like if it was Ebola, and that they would wait for those symptoms before reporting

Participants also reported they would ensure that a fever was due to Ebola, rather than another illness, before seeking care. In the interim they would try to self-medicate or visit local trusted health care facilities. This finding is a potential explanation for why there was a two to three day delay between onset of Ebola symptoms and hospitalization [9].
292.Rationale: The passage directly links the delay in hospitalization to individuals' reluctance to seek care until they were certain their symptoms were due to Ebola, often by waiting for specific symptoms or trying other treatments first. This uncertainty about diagnosis, which may involve misunderstandings about the disease or testing process, is cited as the primary factor for the delay, despite high reported intentions to seek care in surveys.

292.Option: Fear that chlorine spray in ambulances could be lethal contributed to delayed care-seeking despite high intentions to use the response system
Evidence: About one-third of participants expressed the belief that chlorine was harmful to people.
Some participants feared that the chlorine used by ambulance workers was toxic and potentially lethal.
292.Rationale: While fear of chlorine spray was a concern among some participants, the passage does not directly connect this fear to the delay between symptom onset and hospitalization. The delay is primarily attributed to other factors, such as waiting to confirm Ebola diagnosis and self-medication, rather than specific fears about chlorine.

292.Option: Lack of access to transportation to health facilities resulted in delayed hospitalization despite high intentions to seek care
Evidence: Due to inefficiencies in the transportation and reporting systems, returning results on an Ebola test took a minimum of 48 hours [22].
292.Rationale: The passage mentions transportation inefficiencies only in the context of delays in test results, not as a barrier to seeking care or hospitalization. There is no evidence that lack of transportation contributed to the delay in hospitalization; instead, the delay is explained by behavioral and perceptual factors related to care-seeking.

293.Option: Neutralizing antibody titers against the homologous virus are not predictive of protection against heterologous challenge, as the animals were protected despite low cross-reactive titers
Evidence: Animals described here had nAb titers that far surpassed the 1:10 threshold (Fig 4D).
nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).
All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
293.Rationale: The passage indicates that neutralizing antibody titers were high (far above the 1:10 protective threshold) and were cross-reactive between African and Asian lineages, not low. Therefore, the claim that protection occurred despite low cross-reactive titers is contradicted by the data.

293.Option: Protection from heterologous viremia is mediated by T cell responses rather than neutralizing antibodies, as evidenced by the absence of detectable viremia despite low nAb titers
Evidence: nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response [40–43].
Animals described here had nAb titers that far surpassed the 1:10 threshold (Fig 4D).
All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
293.Rationale: While T cell responses were observed, the passage emphasizes neutralizing antibodies as a critical component of protection. Furthermore, nAb titers were high, not low, and the study does not provide evidence that T cells are the primary mediator over nAbs.

293.Option: The presence of neutralizing antibodies at any detectable level is sufficient to prevent viremia in heterologous Zika virus challenge, regardless of titer or cross-reactivity
Evidence: Animals described here had nAb titers that far surpassed the 1:10 threshold (Fig 4D).
For both JE and TBE vaccines a 1:10 PRNT50 nAb titer is regarded as protective in animals and humans [44,45].
293.Rationale: The passage references a protective threshold (1:10) for other flaviviruses and notes that the observed titers far exceeded this threshold. It does not test whether any detectable level is sufficient; it only demonstrates protection at high titers. Therefore, this statement overinterprets the findings.

293.Option: The lack of detectable viremia after heterologous challenge indicates sterilizing immunity, which is likely due to the rapid induction of innate immune responses rather than neutralizing antibodies
Evidence: We did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the immunity was not sterilizing because low-level replication likely occurred, as evidenced by the boost in nAb titers. It does not attribute protection primarily to innate immune responses; instead, it highlights the role of neutralizing antibodies.

293.Option: High and cross-reactive neutralizing antibody titers are strongly associated with protection from detectable viremia during heterologous Zika virus challenge
Evidence: Animals described here had nAb titers that far surpassed the 1:10 threshold (Fig 4D).
nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).
All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).
nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response.
293.Rationale: The study demonstrates that macaques with high neutralizing antibody titers (well above the established protective threshold) that are cross-reactive between African and Asian ZIKV lineages were completely protected from detectable viremia upon heterologous challenge. This strong association is supported by statistical significance and the authors' emphasis on nAbs as a key correlate of protection.

294.Option: The association remained significant even after adjusting for infant preterm birth and low birth weight, which are known mediators of the relationship between maternal infection and neurodevelopment
Evidence: sensitivity analyses performed by further adjusting for infant preterm status and weight-for-age, yielded similar results
Further adjustment for LBW (not preterm birth) and weight-for-age showed similar conclusions in the sensitivity analyses.
294.Rationale: Adjusting for infant preterm birth and low birth weight addresses potential mediators, not confounders; these factors are likely in the causal pathway between maternal infection and neurodevelopment, so their adjustment does not rule out confounding by maternal anemia or iron deficiency.

294.Option: The study used a validated neurodevelopmental assessment tool (MSEL) administered by trained research nurses, reducing measurement error and increasing confidence in the observed association
Evidence: trained research nurses individually assessed the cognitive function of 635 one-year-old infants using the Mullen Scales of Early Learning (MSEL)
Detailed quality assurance and reliability of assessment have already been published.
294.Rationale: While using a validated tool and trained nurses reduces measurement error in the outcome assessment, it does not address confounding by maternal anemia or iron deficiency, which could affect both exposure and outcome independently.

294.Option: The study adjusted for malaria co-infection during pregnancy, which is a known risk factor for anemia and poor neurodevelopment, thereby reducing the likelihood of confounding by iron deficiency
Evidence: malaria has also been assessed several times during pregnancy allowing for the study of the impact of malaria-helminth co-infection on child development
maternal malarial infection was not statistically significantly associated with ELC and gross motor scores
294.Rationale: Although malaria co-infection was assessed, it was not statistically significant and may not have been included in the final multivariate models; even if adjusted for, it does not directly account for all forms of maternal anemia or iron deficiency, which can arise from other causes.

294.Option: The association persisted after adjusting for multiple socioeconomic and maternal health confounders, suggesting it is not fully explained by unmeasured anemia
Evidence: The difference in mean ELC scores between children whose mothers were infected with helminths at first ANC visit and those whose mothers were not infected with any helminth remained significant after adjusting for maternal education, child sex and HOME score (p = 0.013)

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028)
294.Rationale: The association remained significant after adjusting for multiple socioeconomic and maternal health confounders that are correlated with risk factors for anemia, suggesting that unmeasured maternal anemia is unlikely to fully explain the observed association, as these confounders capture some of the variability in anemia risk.

294.Option: The association was strongest for hookworm infection, which is the most common helminth species associated with iron deficiency, suggesting a biologically plausible causal pathway
Evidence: hookworm infections were the most prevalent (9.5%)

mothers who were infected with hookworms during the first ANC visit had children who scored less in the gross motor scale, -4.9 (95% CI: -8.6 to-1.1), compared to those whose mothers were never infected with hookworms at first ANC visit
helminth infection especially with hookworms is a known risk factor for ID
294.Rationale: The strength of association for hookworm infection indicates biological plausibility but does not rule out confounding by maternal anemia; in fact, it may suggest that anemia is a mediator in the causal pathway rather than a confounder.

296.Option: The observation that minor variants are primarily found at frequencies below 1%, which is below the detection limit of Sanger sequencing, supports the idea that immune pressure generates rare, transient antigenic variants that are quickly eliminated
Evidence: The intrastrain heterogeneous sequences were valid if the following conditions were simultaneously verified for any variant sequence: 1) being supported by at least fifty reads and 2) displaying a frequency above 1%.
the major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range
296.Rationale: The passage sets a 1% threshold for considering variants valid, and most minor variants are reported in the 1-5% range, not below 1%. Therefore, the option's claim about variants primarily below 1% is not supported. Additionally, while low-frequency variants may be transient, the passage does not directly link this to immune pressure driving diversity; instead, it suggests that variants emerge and may disappear if not advantageous.

296.Option: The high frequency of a single predominant sequence in most V regions, especially V1, indicates that immune pressure stabilizes a conserved antigenic profile early in infection
Evidence: there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
V1 presented a strong shared sequence capacity.
296.Rationale: The passage shows that predominant sequences are highly frequent in most samples, suggesting conservation. However, this conservation might indicate a lack of strong immune pressure for diversification or that these regions are less variable. The hypothesis is that immune pressure drives antigenic diversity, so evidence of conservation does not support this; instead, it could imply the opposite.

296.Option: The increase in frequency of minor variants in V6, a region with the highest genetic diversity, during early infection suggests immune-driven selection for antigenic escape
Evidence: the frequency of the predominant sequence in V6 of X-6 was even lower at 20.8%.
the frequency of the predominant sequence in V6 was particularly low (Fig 2A), suggesting that V6 may be the first affected region and is involved in immune evasion during the course of infection.

the distribution pattern of these variants from these samples may suggest that with disease progression or increasing immunity, the balance of the original sequence distribution was broken and some V regions (e.g., V2, V5, V6 and V7) began to change. As a result, a minor variant (or a new variant) became advantageous and its frequency gradually increased, ultimately replacing the original predominant sequence, which further promoted the antigenic diversity of TprK for T. pallidum to escape immune clearance
296.Rationale: The passage directly links lower frequency of predominant sequences in V6 (and other regions) to immune evasion and antigenic escape. It suggests that as immunity increases, minor variants become advantageous and increase in frequency, promoting antigenic diversity. This strongly supports the hypothesis that immune pressure drives antigenic diversity during early infection.

296.Option: The presence of multiple length variants in V3, V6, and V7, which are the most variable regions, indicates that structural changes in TprK are driven by immune pressure to evade host recognition
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.
the rampant diversity of tprK could be regulated by a strict gene conversion mechanism to avoid yielding an abnormal detrimental antigen.
296.Rationale: While length variants exist in these regions, the passage attributes this diversity to a regulated gene conversion mechanism that avoids frameshifts. It does not explicitly state that immune pressure drives these structural changes; the connection is inferred but not as direct as the evidence for immune-driven selection in V6. Therefore, this option does not most strongly support the hypothesis.

296.Option: The high inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4, despite overall tprK variability, suggests that immune pressure is strongest in these regions, leading to conserved antigenic profiles across patients
Evidence: V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity.
the occurrence of the consistent sequence in V1 and V4 could reach five strains and six strains, respectively.
the highly stable shared peptide of V1 and V4 across all the strains would likely be a potential target for vaccine development.
296.Rationale: Inter-population redundancy indicates conservation of sequences across patients, which might be due to functional constraints or immune selection for conserved epitopes. However, this conservation does not support the driving of antigenic diversity; instead, it suggests that these regions are less diverse and possibly under different selective pressures. The hypothesis focuses on immune pressure driving diversity, not conservation.

297.Option: Implement population-wide door-to-door screening every six months to detect all cases of PKDL and leprosy in the region
Evidence: Active case finding through door-to-door screening is probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable.
Our teams spent more than one year, with interruptions, to screen a population of approximately 33,000 and were able to examine only three-quarters of those.
297.Rationale: While door-to-door screening is effective, the passage clearly indicates it is not sustainable due to high resource requirements and time consumption. Implementing it every six months is unrealistic and does not specifically account for spatial patterns of leprosy transmission.

297.Option: Prioritize screening for leprosy in households with a history of VL to detect potential PKDL cases due to shared risk factors
Evidence: The three PKDL cases were all from different villages.
Among leprosy patients, strong spatial clustering was apparent at neighborhood level.
297.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that households with a history of VL are at increased risk for leprosy. Leprosy clusters independently around other leprosy cases, and PKDL is rare and not necessarily co-located with leprosy. This strategy would likely miss leprosy clusters not associated with VL history and may not efficiently detect PKDL.

297.Option: Use passive case finding through health facility reporting to identify new cases of PKDL and leprosy in areas with high leprosy prevalence
Evidence: Considerable underreporting of PKDL is suspected.
skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
297.Rationale: Passive case finding is implied to be insufficient due to underreporting and missed cases. The passage advocates for active strategies like skin camps to improve detection. Passive reporting does not actively account for spatial patterns of leprosy and may fail to identify hidden transmission risks.

297.Option: Conduct targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases and follow up past VL patients for PKDL to detect hidden transmission risks
Evidence: Targeting active case finding for leprosy to household members of cases and neighbors seems rational
For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.
rate ratios of 6.3 (95% CI 1.9–21.0) and 3.6 (95% CI 1.3–10.2) respectively for those living in the same household and neighbors at less than 25 meters of a leprosy patient.
297.Rationale: This strategy directly uses the spatial clustering pattern of leprosy (high risk within households and <25 meters) to target leprosy screening effectively. Additionally, following up past VL patients addresses the hidden reservoir of PKDL, which can sustain VL transmission. This aligns with the passage's recommended combination approach and accounts for both diseases efficiently.

297.Option: Establish skin camps in villages with the highest historical VL incidence to detect PKDL and leprosy cases through opportunistic screening
Evidence: skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
A combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach.
297.Rationale: While skin camps in high historical VL incidence villages can detect PKDL and leprosy opportunistically and are part of the recommended combination, this option does not explicitly account for the fine-scale spatial patterns of leprosy (clustering within villages). It targets villages based on VL history but may miss leprosy cases in areas without high VL incidence or fail to leverage leprosy clustering for more efficient case finding.

298.Option: The route of administration of the monoclonal antibody, such as intraperitoneal versus intravenous delivery
Evidence: were injected ip with the indicated amount of ZEBOV GP-specific MAbs in 100 µl PBS
the guinea pigs were treated i.p. with 1 ml of the MAb diluted in PBS
298.Rationale: The passage only describes intraperitoneal administration for both mice and guinea pigs, with no comparison to other routes or evidence that route of delivery strongly influences the likelihood of complete protection when treatment is delayed.

298.Option: The presence of a neutralizing antibody response in the host's endogenous immune system at the time of treatment
Evidence: Both T and B cell immunity was analysed and it was believed that a strong early humoral immune response may have been a factor in survival
the passive transfer of immune sera or whole blood was tested but its effectiveness is still controversial
298.Rationale: The passage discusses endogenous immune responses in the context of natural infection but does not link the host's pre-existing neutralizing antibodies to the efficacy of delayed monoclonal antibody treatment. The study focuses on passive transfer of exogenous antibodies, not endogenous responses.

298.Option: The total dose of monoclonal antibodies administered, regardless of timing relative to infection
Evidence: A dose response was observed, and some MAbs were more potent than others for a given dose
Delaying the treatment to 3 dpi resulted in no survival for all MAbs except for 4G7 (10% survival rate)
298.Rationale: While dose affects efficacy, the passage demonstrates that timing is more critical for delayed treatment, as even effective doses fail to provide complete protection when administered too late (e.g., at 3 dpi).

298.Option: The specific epitope targeted by the monoclonal antibody on the Ebola virus glycoprotein
Evidence: Targeting multiple regions of GP appears to be a successful strategy
Each of these MAbs binds to different regions of GP1,2
298.Rationale: Although epitope specificity can influence antibody function, the passage consistently highlights timing as the key factor for complete protection in delayed treatment scenarios, with efficacy sharply declining after 2 days post-infection irrespective of epitope.

298.Option: The specific timing of treatment relative to infection onset, particularly whether it occurs within 2 days post-infection, is the most critical factor for achieving complete protection
Evidence: Treating the mice on 1 or 2 dpi was the most effective treatment time
Delaying the treatment to 3 dpi resulted in no survival for all MAbs except for 4G7 (10% survival rate)
the neutralizing MAb combination improved survival in all treatment protocols with the 2 dpi treatment protocol being the most effective
This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected
298.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the timing of monoclonal antibody administration is crucial for complete protection, with optimal efficacy within 2 days post-infection and a significant drop in survival when treatment is delayed beyond this window.

299.Option: Select the cut-off value that corresponds to the lowest antibody level observed in any confirmed VL case to ensure no false negatives
Evidence: With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
False negative results were found for 2 (2.06%) of 97 VL samples.
299.Rationale: Setting the cut-off at the lowest antibody level in cases would indeed achieve 100% sensitivity, but this is not the method used in the study, and it would likely compromise specificity. The study actually had a few false negatives, indicating that the chosen cut-off did not correspond to the minimum observed level; rather, it was chosen via ROC curve analysis to balance sensitivity and specificity.

299.Option: Use a receiver-operating characteristic (ROC) curve to identify the optimal cut-off point that maximizes both sensitivity and specificity
Evidence: A receiver-operator characteristic (ROC) curve for ELISA was constructed to determine the cut-off value with 95% confidence intervals (CI) that discriminate between VL-positive and -negative urine samples.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
299.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that an ROC curve was used to determine the cut-off value for the ELISA. This is the standard and most appropriate method in diagnostic test evaluation to find the threshold that best discriminates between infected and uninfected individuals by maximizing both sensitivity and specificity.

299.Option: Set the cut-off value at the mean antibody level of the control group plus two standard deviations to minimize false positives
Evidence: With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
The AUC obtained for the test was 0.9984, depicting its potential to discriminate cases with and without the disease and comprises a high degree of agreement (κ = 0.97) with the gold standard test.
299.Rationale: While using mean plus two standard deviations is a common method for defining normal ranges in some laboratory tests, it is not the method employed in this study. The study used an ROC curve to determine the cut-off, which is more appropriate for diagnostic tests as it considers both sensitivity and specificity rather than just minimizing false positives.

299.Option: Use the median antibody level of the VL-positive group as the cut-off to ensure equal distribution of values above and below the threshold
Evidence: With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
Urine from VL patients illustrated significantly (P <0.0001) stronger recognition against LAg with a high degree of specificity.
299.Rationale: Using the median of the positive group does not consider the distribution of antibody levels in the control group and is not a standard method for determining a diagnostic cut-off. The study instead used an ROC curve, which accounts for both case and control distributions to optimize discrimination.

300.Option: Respondents may have misreported their feeding behavior due to social desirability bias, especially among those with negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Some unexpected findings also arose from the current survey. For example, although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage does not attribute the discrepancy to misreporting or social desirability bias. Instead, it presents the feeding reports as factual and explains the inconsistency with religious beliefs. There is no mention of respondents misrepresenting their feeding practices due to social desirability.

300.Option: The inconsistency between feeding and attitudes is due to a lack of understanding about the transmission of rabies through licks and scratches, which leads to underestimation of risk
Evidence: less than half (50, 40.7%) were aware that it could also be transmitted through licks/scratches.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52]
300.Rationale: While lack of knowledge about transmission via licks/scratches is noted, the passage does not link this specific knowledge gap to the discrepancy between feeding practices and attitudes. The explanation provided for the inconsistency is religious beliefs, not underestimation of risk due to incomplete transmission knowledge.

300.Option: Religious beliefs promoting animal care override risk awareness, leading to feeding despite negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Some unexpected findings also arose from the current survey. For example, although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them. Similarly, the respondents who fed them surprisingly had a lower acceptance for FRD (OR 0.08).

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage directly addresses the discrepancy: many respondents feed FRD despite having low acceptance (negative attitudes). It explains this inconsistency by pointing to religious convictions (Hinduism) that promote animal care, which override their negative perceptions and lead to feeding behavior.

300.Option: Higher socio-economic status is associated with greater access to information about rabies, leading to more consistent reporting of feeding practices and attitudes
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005)
Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.
300.Rationale: The passage shows that higher SES is associated with more positive attitudes and more feeding, so their reporting is consistent (positive attitudes align with feeding). However, the question asks about the discrepancy between feeding and attitudes, which is observed in the overall sample (feeders having lower acceptance). The passage does not suggest that higher SES leads to more consistent reporting as an explanation for the discrepancy; instead, it highlights religious beliefs as the key factor.

300.Option: The discrepancy arises because respondents from lower socio-economic groups are more likely to feed dogs out of necessity, while higher SES groups feed them out of compassion, leading to divergent attitudes
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005)
Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.
It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions
300.Rationale: The passage indicates that lower SES groups are less likely to feed FRD, not more likely out of necessity. Higher SES groups feed more and have positive attitudes. The discrepancy (feeding despite negative attitudes) is not explained by SES-based motivational differences; rather, religious beliefs cut across SES and are offered as the explanation. The passage does not mention feeding out of necessity for lower SES.

301.Option: The pathogen is transmitted directly from infected fleas to their offspring and persists across life stages without requiring vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas
the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas
301.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that R. felis is passed generationally in fleas and maintained transovarially and transstadially, meaning it can be sustained in flea populations without needing vertebrate hosts. This directly supports the hypothesis of exclusive arthropod maintenance without a vertebrate reservoir.

301.Option: The pathogen causes severe neurological disease in humans, indicating a high pathogenic potential that likely requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations
Evidence: In this report we describe the identification of R. felis from the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease
Rickettsia felis is increasingly recognized as a human pathogen, often causing febrile illness
301.Rationale: While R. felis can cause severe or fatal disease in humans, this does not provide evidence that it requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations for maintenance. Many arthropod-borne pathogens are maintained exclusively in arthropod cycles despite causing human disease, and the passage does not link pathogenicity to vertebrate reservoir dependence.

301.Option: The pathogen was first isolated from cat fleas, indicating that fleas are the primary and exclusive vector, which supports a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts
Evidence: Rickettsia felis was first identified as a R. typhi-like organism in the midgut epithelium of a commercial colony of cat fleas, Ctenocephalides felis [1]
301.Rationale: First isolation from fleas suggests fleas are involved in transmission, but it does not confirm that they are the exclusive vector or that the cycle is independent of vertebrate hosts. The passage notes that the exact mechanisms, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear, so this option overstates the evidence.

301.Option: The pathogen is not detected in human blood samples, suggesting it is not adapted to vertebrate hosts and thus likely maintained solely in arthropods
Evidence: Because Rickettsia invade endothelial cells, DNA is typically scarce in sera or CSF [23]
Insufficient sera were available from these two cases for Rickettsia PCR
301.Rationale: Scarcity of DNA in blood or lack of testing does not imply that the pathogen is not adapted to vertebrate hosts. Rickettsia are intracellular bacteria, and low DNA levels in blood are common; this does not support exclusive arthropod maintenance. The passage does not show conclusive absence in blood.

301.Option: The pathogen has been detected in multiple arthropod species, including mosquitoes and fleas, suggesting a broad vector range that supports transmission without vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes
301.Rationale: Detection in multiple arthropod species indicates a broad vector range, but this alone does not strongly support exclusive maintenance in arthropods without vertebrate reservoirs. The passage does not rule out vertebrate involvement, and broad vector range could still coexist with vertebrate reservoirs.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate of 41.3% in elderly dengue patients justifies the use of any available laboratory test for early risk stratification
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients)
During the 2015 dengue outbreak in Taiwan, the patients with severe dengue in ICUs were characterized by old age, multiple comorbidities, and a high CFR.
302.Rationale: While the high case fatality rate underscores the need for effective risk stratification, it does not specifically justify the use of a single laboratory test over multi-parameter scoring systems. The passage does not state that the high CFR alone justifies using any available test; rather, it emphasizes identifying specific independent prognostic factors like APTT for risk stratification.

302.Option: Prolonged APTT at ICU admission remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after accounting for multi-parameter severity scores
Evidence: APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality
APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that prolonged APTT is an independent predictor of mortality even after adjusting for multi-parameter scores like APACHE II and SOFA. This supports the use of a single laboratory test for early risk stratification because it provides prognostic information beyond complex scoring systems, potentially offering a simpler and more accessible tool in resource-limited settings.

302.Option: High APACHE II and SOFA scores are strongly associated with in-hospital fatality and therefore should be prioritized for early risk stratification in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables.
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
302.Rationale: Although APACHE II and SOFA scores are strongly associated with fatality (higher AUCs), the passage does not argue that they should be prioritized over a single laboratory test. In fact, it highlights the independent prognostic value of APTT despite these scores. The question asks for what supports using a single test, so this option argues against that by advocating for multi-parameter scores.

302.Option: APTT prolongation is more sensitive than APACHE II and SOFA scores for detecting early signs of organ failure in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).

Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT.
302.Rationale: The passage does not claim that APTT is more sensitive than APACHE II or SOFA scores for detecting organ failure. The AUC values indicate that APTT has a lower discriminatory ability (though not significantly different), and the scores actually have higher AUCs. Therefore, this statement is not supported by the evidence.

302.Option: The presence of acute respiratory failure at ICU admission is a stronger predictor of fatality than APTT prolongation and should be used for early risk stratification
Evidence: In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s.
302.Rationale: While acute respiratory failure was significant in univariate analysis, it was not retained as an independent predictor in the final multivariate model, whereas APTT prolongation was. This indicates that APTT is a stronger independent predictor than respiratory failure. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

304.Option: Use Bayesian inference with a fixed geographic origin for each node to estimate the timing of transmission events
Evidence: Bayesian analysis was used to estimate the dates of the nodes in the phylogenetic trees.
The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].
304.Rationale: The passage uses Bayesian inference for dating nodes, but for inferring geographic origin at ancestral nodes, it employs stochastic character mapping with posterior probability estimation, not fixing geographic origin. The method described accounts for uncertainty through simulations, unlike a fixed origin approach.

304.Option: Apply maximum parsimony to reconstruct the most likely geographic origin of ancestral nodes based on the fewest transmission events
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.
304.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes using stochastic character mapping, not maximum parsimony, to infer geographic origins. Stochastic mapping involves simulations and posterior probabilities to account for uncertainty, whereas parsimony does not inherently incorporate such uncertainty estimation.

304.Option: Use stochastic character mapping with posterior probability estimation via multiple simulations to infer geographic origin at ancestral nodes
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips. The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
The transmission routes for the S. Typhimurium clusters were inferred by stochastic mapping of the geographic origin of each isolate onto the tree [48].
304.Rationale: This option directly matches the method described in the passage for inferring the likely geographic origin of ancestral nodes while accounting for uncertainty. The use of multiple simulations and posterior probability estimation aligns with the need to infer historical migration and population dynamics from genetic lineages' geographic distributions across evolutionary time.

304.Option: Use a parsimony-based ancestral state reconstruction with bootstrapping to assess confidence in geographic origin estimates
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.
304.Rationale: The passage does not mention parsimony-based reconstruction with bootstrapping; instead, it uses stochastic character mapping with simulations and posterior probabilities. This method is specifically designed to infer geographic origins with uncertainty, which bootstrapping in parsimony might not fully capture in the same way.

304.Option: Perform a likelihood ratio test to compare geographic origin hypotheses at ancestral nodes using a time-calibrated phylogeny
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.
304.Rationale: The passage does not describe using likelihood ratio tests for geographic origin inference. Instead, stochastic mapping with posterior probabilities is used to directly infer origins at ancestral nodes, which is more appropriate for accounting for uncertainty in transmission events without comparing specific hypotheses via tests.

306.Option: The use of a quantitative ELISA assay to measure circulating filarial antigen levels, enabling detection of changes in adult worm burden over time
Evidence: The CFA level was analysed using the OD values generated from the ELISA assay of plasma samples
This is a benefit of using a quantitative ELISA instead of a qualitative measurement.
306.Rationale: While the quantitative ELISA provides valuable data on antigen levels and changes in worm burden, it is a measurement tool that enhances sensitivity and detail but does not directly strengthen causal attribution to the treatment campaign. Attribution requires methodological design elements that control for confounding and bias, which are not addressed by the assay itself.

306.Option: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region to compare changes in filarial prevalence over time
306.Rationale: The passage does not mention any control group from a non-treatment region. The study is focused solely on the treatment area in Kyela district, with comparisons made over time within the same population. Without a control group, it is difficult to rule out secular trends or external factors, so this feature is absent and does not contribute to confidence in attribution.

306.Option: The use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements over time, which controls for both sampling bias and age-related confounding
Evidence: In a second analysis, only the subgroup of individuals who participated in the activities in 2009, 2011 and 2019 were considered
The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study.
306.Rationale: The fixed cohort design with repeated measurements on the same individuals over time minimizes sampling bias and allows for within-person comparisons, which strengthens internal validity. By following the same cohort, changes in infection status can be more confidently linked to the treatment campaign rather than to differences in sample composition or age-related confounding, as age changes are tracked within individuals.

306.Option: The statistical adjustment for age, sex, and occupation in multivariable regression models to control for potential confounders
Evidence: Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study
we performed uni- and multi-variable log link binomial regression analyses with robust variance estimates in order to assess potential risk factors for LF infection
306.Rationale: Statistical adjustment is a valuable method to control for measured confounders, but it does not address unmeasured confounding or biases such as sampling bias. In this context, the fixed cohort design provides a stronger foundation for attribution by ensuring consistent measurement over time and reducing bias, whereas adjustment alone may not fully account for all factors influencing the observed reduction.

307.Option: The significant association between male sex and leptospiral IgM positivity, especially in the 20–29 age group, suggests occupational exposure to infected individuals, supporting direct transmission in high-risk professions
Evidence: The significant associations with male sex are consistent with the fact that most occupations at high risk (farmers, miners, recreational activities in muddy grounds, exposures to flood water, building workers) for leptospirosis are often associated with males.
307.Rationale: The passage links male sex to occupations involving exposure to contaminated environments like flood water or soil, not to direct exposure to infected humans. Leptospirosis is primarily transmitted through environmental contact with animal urine, not person-to-person. Thus, this option incorrectly suggests human-to-human transmission and does not support the environmental exposure hypothesis.

307.Option: The seasonal peak of cases during the rainy season, particularly in urban areas, indicates that transmission is driven by increased human mobility and social interaction, supporting a hypothesis of person-to-person spread
Evidence: We found that cases occur year-round in the DRC, with a higher proportion during the rainy season.
The seasonality of leptospirosis has been described in most parts of the world, with a significantly higher incidence during rainy months, in tropical areas.
Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains.
307.Rationale: The passage associates the rainy season with increased flooding and environmental contamination, which are known risk factors for leptospirosis transmission via water or soil. It does not link the seasonal peak to human mobility or social interaction. Therefore, this option misattributes the cause to person-to-person spread rather than environmental exposure.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas, particularly in Kinshasa, suggests that the disease is spreading through dense human populations, indicating possible person-to-person transmission
Evidence: Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains. It is described that rapid urbanization, overcrowding, and natural disasters are risk factors promoting the transmission of leptospirosis.

Leptospirosis is a zoonotic disease with no significant person-to-person transmission. Thus, this option incorrectly infers human-to-human spread.

307.Option: The presence of Leptospira IgM in 14 provinces across the DRC, including both urban and rural areas, indicates widespread human-to-human transmission across diverse populations and regions
Evidence: Subjects with anti-Leptospira IgM antibodies were found in 14 provinces with different type of climate, suggesting a broad distribution and circulation of Leptospira spp. in the country.
307.Rationale: Widespread presence across provinces reflects the environmental circulation of Leptospira due to varied climates and animal reservoirs, not human-to-human transmission. The passage does not suggest person-to-person spread as a mode of transmission; instead, it emphasizes environmental exposure. Hence, this option misinterprets the evidence.

307.Option: The clustering of cases among young adult males in urban areas during the rainy season, coinciding with flooding and poor sanitation, aligns with environmental exposure pathways rather than person-to-person transmission
Evidence: The majority of these Leptospira IgM cases were among young adult males (20–29 years) living in an urban setting.
We found that cases occur year-round in the DRC, with a higher proportion during the rainy season.
Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains.

The significant associations with male sex are consistent with the fact that most occupations at high risk (farmers, miners, recreational activities in muddy grounds, exposures to flood water, building workers) for leptospirosis are often associated with males.
307.Rationale: This option directly connects the epidemiological patterns—demographic clustering, seasonal variation, and urban conditions—to environmental factors like flooding and occupational exposure to contaminated water or soil. These are established transmission routes for leptospirosis, which is not typically spread person-to-person. The passage provides evidence supporting this interpretation, making it the strongest factor for the environmental exposure hypothesis.

308.Option: The combination of waning insecticidal efficacy and reduced use of complementary protective behaviors likely led to increased dengue exposure despite high curtain usage
Evidence: the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed. a contributing factor may have been a false sense of security amongst the participants in the intervention arm; i.e., perhaps the presence of the highly visible ITCs created a belief in their protective power among the treated households, who subsequently did not employ any additional measures typically used to avoid exposure to mosquito bites.

Participants that had received ITCs commented that when the curtains were first hung, the household reduced their use of mosquito repellents and stopped fumigating because they felt that it was no longer necessary due to the presence of the ITCs
308.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies both waning insecticidal efficacy (due to rapid loss of insecticide) and reduced complementary protective behaviors (false sense of security leading to discontinuation of other measures) as key factors that together likely contributed to the higher seroconversion in the intervention arm, explaining increased exposure despite high curtain usage.

308.Option: The initial entomological improvements observed in the intervention arm were not sustained due to the failure of insecticide-treated curtains to maintain efficacy over time, leading to increased dengue transmission
Evidence: Although entomological indicators appeared to be lower in the intervention arm, most notably at the first follow-up survey, the differences were not statistically significant and were not sustained over the course of the study.
entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study

308.Option: The re-treatment of insecticide-treated curtains with deltamethrin likely disrupted the protective effect by causing a temporary reduction in insecticidal activity, increasing dengue exposure during the follow-up period

309.Option: The similarity of final clinical response rates between Bangladesh and India supports the generalizability of the results, suggesting that the 6-month clinical endpoint is a valid proxy for parasitological cure in both settings
Evidence: The final clinical response rate (94.2%) is similar to the final clinical response rate observed in the Phase III (94.2% final response) and Phase IV (94.6% final response) studies of PMIM in India [4,5].

The passage explicitly states that parasitological confirmation (e.g., splenic aspiration) was not feasible due to resource limitations, so the clinical endpoint was used out of necessity, not because it was validated. Therefore, this factor does not critically influence interpretation of long-term success; rather, it highlights a limitation.

309.Option: The use of a 6-month follow-up window is sufficient to detect late relapses because the majority of relapses occur within the first 3 months after treatment, minimizing the risk of underestimating true cure rates

Evidence: The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. Patients were not required to return for follow-up, but were encouraged to return if problems arose during this 30-day window.
Data on occurrences of PKDL cases after end of treatment were not collected.
309.Rationale: In a resource-limited, outpatient setting, the lack of parasitological confirmation (e.g., splenic aspiration) means that clinical signs alone may not detect subclinical or early relapses. This directly and critically influences the interpretation of long-term treatment success, as the reported cure rates may be overestimated due to diagnostic limitations and incomplete follow-up.

309.Option: The high compliance rate of 98.3% ensures that the final clinical response rate accurately reflects the true effectiveness of paromomycin, minimizing bias from treatment non-adherence
Evidence: A total of 118/120 subjects (98.3%) were compliant with treatment (21 daily injections over no more than 22 days).
Patients were not required to return for follow-up, but were encouraged to return if problems arose during this 30-day window.

309.Rationale: While high compliance reduces bias from non-adherence, it does not ensure that the final clinical response rate accurately reflects true effectiveness. Other biases, such as loss to follow-up (since follow-up was not mandatory) and reliance on clinical endpoints without parasitological confirmation, can still distort the estimate. The passage indicates that follow-up was passive, which may lead to incomplete outcome assessment. Thus, compliance alone is not the most critical factor for interpreting long-term success.

310.Option: Compare the proportion of patients with ECG abnormalities at 5-year intervals post-treatment to assess changes in protection over time
Evidence: During the follow-up period, alterations in routine ECG were observed in 11/234 (4.7%, 95% CI [2–7.4%]).
median follow up time of 9.8 years (6–20.2 years, IQR 7.5–12.4)
310.Rationale: This approach ignores censoring and varying follow-up times. The passage reports a cumulative proportion over the entire follow-up period, not stratified by time intervals. With varying follow-up durations, comparing proportions at fixed intervals without accounting for person-time or censoring can lead to biased estimates and does not properly assess changes in risk over time.

310.Option: Compare the incidence of cardiac abnormalities across time intervals since treatment to detect potential waning of protection
Evidence: median follow up time of 9.8 years (6–20.2 years, IQR 7.5–12.4)
with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up

Comparing these rates across intervals directly tests whether the risk of cardiac abnormalities increases over time, which would indicate waning protection. The cohort's long-term follow-up with varying durations makes incidence-based methods appropriate.

310.Option: Use survival analysis to estimate the time to first cardiac abnormality after treatment, accounting for censoring due to loss to follow-up
Evidence: The kinetics of T. cruzi serum antibodies were analyzed using survival analysis.
310.Rationale: While survival analysis appropriately handles time-to-event data and censoring, merely estimating the time to first cardiac abnormality (e.g., with Kaplan-Meier curves) is descriptive and does not directly assess whether protection diminishes over time. To evaluate waning, one must specifically test for changes in the hazard function over time, which requires additional modeling steps not implied by this option.

310.Option: Calculate the odds ratio of cardiac abnormalities in patients treated within the first year of infection compared to those treated later, using a case-control design within the cohort
Evidence: Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.
The median age at diagnosis was 62.53 months (range 1–202 months, IQR 11–116)

The passage notes variation in age at diagnosis, but the question specifically asks about diminishing protection over time after treatment. Additionally, using a case-control design within the cohort may be inefficient and does not directly model time-varying risk.

310.Option: Perform a time-dependent Cox regression model with treatment duration as a time-varying covariate to assess changes in risk over time
Evidence: Bz (5–8 mg/kg in 2 or 3 doses) or Nf (10–12 mg/kg in 2 or 3 doses) was prescribed for 60–90 days.
Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.
310.Rationale: Treatment duration is a fixed characteristic (60–90 days), not a time-varying covariate. Moreover, in the absence of an untreated control group, a Cox model with treatment as a covariate is not applicable. To assess waning protection, one would need to model the hazard as a function of time since treatment, but this option misstates the relevant time variable and overlooks the lack of a comparison group.

311.Option: The genetic similarity between Bhutanese DENV-1 strains and a specific clade of DENV-1 from northern India strongly indicates recent cross-border transmission
Evidence: Phylogenetic characterization of DENV-1 revealed that they belong to genotype V, and were probably imported from India.

All 33 Bhutan sequences group to genotype V, using the classification of Weaver et al [21], and were located in the same group as sequences from northern India, categorized as clade IX by Dash et al [22].

Most of the Bhutan sequences grouped with the 2008–2009 northern Indian sequences, indicating that the 2013–2014 dengue viruses from Bhutan mainly originated from 2008–2009 northern India sub-clade viruses.
311.Rationale: The passage provides direct molecular evidence through phylogenetic analysis showing that Bhutanese DENV-1 strains cluster specifically with clades from northern India. This genetic similarity is explicitly cited as evidence for importation from India, making it a strong support for cross-border transmission.

311.Option: The detection of DENV-1 in Bhutan during 2013–2014, which is the same serotype that was dominant in India after 2006, supports the hypothesis of cross-border transmission due to temporal concordance
Evidence: DENV-1 prevalence has increased in India since 2006.

Similarly, in this study, DENV-1 was predominant in 2013–2014, further supporting the notion that dengue epidemiological patterns in Bhutan and India are closely linked with likely exchange of strains between the two countries.

311.Option: The seasonal peak of cases during the monsoon months aligns with the transmission patterns in India, suggesting that the virus is imported from India during periods of high transmission
Evidence: In both years, the number of suspected and laboratory confirmed cases peaked during the summer months (June-September), which also corresponds to the monsoon season in Bhutan.
Studies have shown a correlation between rainfall, temperature and humidity with serologically confirmed dengue.
311.Rationale: While seasonal peaks are noted, the passage attributes them to local climatic conditions favorable for mosquito breeding, not to importation from India. No direct link is made between seasonal alignment and cross-border transmission; it is presented as a general ecological pattern.

311.Option: The high proportion of cases among young adults aged 19–35 years suggests that the virus is newly introduced, as this age group is more likely to travel across borders and bring in infections
Evidence: Age of patients in this study ranged from 2 to 77 years but young adults, 19–35 years, accounted for the largest age group of suspected as well as laboratory confirmed cases.
the majority of cases being adults suggest that there are still many dengue-naïve or dengue monotypic individuals among the adult population in Bhutan.
311.Rationale: The passage explains the age distribution in terms of population susceptibility (many dengue-naïve adults) and potential shifts due to vector control, but it does not mention travel behavior or link this age group to cross-border movement. The hypothesis that young adults travel more and import infections is not supported by the passage.

311.Option: The co-circulation of multiple DENV serotypes in India and the detection of DENV-1, DENV-2, and DENV-3 in Bhutan suggest that the virus is being introduced from India due to the shared serotype diversity
Evidence: Bhutan shares a 700 km border with India, which continues to report co-circulation of all 4 DENV serotypes with increasing frequency.
Of 58 positive cases, 53 (91.4%) were DENV-1, 3 (5.2%) DENV-2 and 2 (3.4%) DENV-3; no DENV-4 was detected.
this study demonstrated ... We established DENV-1 as the dominant of at least three DENV serotypes currently circulating in Bhutan.
311.Rationale: Although both countries have multiple serotypes, the passage does not explicitly state that shared serotype diversity is evidence of introduction. The strongest evidence provided is genetic similarity for DENV-1; for other serotypes, no genetic data are given to support importation. The co-circulation could also result from local transmission sustained over time.

312.Option: The spatial clustering of high-prevalence sites in Ese-Odo, despite uniform PC implementation, indicates that individual-level risk factors such as age and sex are the main drivers of transmission differences.
Evidence: Spatial analysis revealed that in Ese-Odo, more than 60% of sampled sites had prevalence rates ranging from 26% to 81%, with these high-prevalence areas widely dispersed.

312.Option: The higher prevalence of moderate and heavy infections in Ese-Odo, despite effective PC, indicates that individual-level compliance with medication was lower in that area compared to Irele and Ile-Oluji.
Evidence: To date, an average of five effective PC rounds with therapeutic coverage exceeding 75% has been achieved.
The three LGAs included in this assessment have met the minimum requirement of 5–6 effective PC rounds.
312.Rationale: PC coverage was uniformly effective across all LGAs, with therapeutic coverage exceeding 75%, so differences in infection burden cannot be explained by varying compliance with medication. Instead, structural conditions like WASH access are highlighted as drivers.

312.Option: The significant reduction in prevalence in Irele and Ile-Oluji, despite similar baseline endemicity levels, suggests that individual-level adherence to hygiene practices was more consistent in those areas compared to Ese-Odo.
Evidence: In Irele, the overall prevalence of any STH was 9.7% ... indicating an 81% reduction from the baseline prevalence of 50.9%
In Ile-Oluji, the overall STH prevalence was 6.4% ... a 72% decrease from the baseline prevalence of 23%
Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs
312.Rationale: While reductions occurred, the passage attributes these to the combined effect of PC and better structural WASH conditions in Irele and Ile-Oluji, not solely to individual adherence. Ese-Odo's poor WASH infrastructure is emphasized as a key barrier, indicating structural drivers rather than individual behavior alone.

312.Option: The persistence of high prevalence in Ese-Odo despite effective preventive chemotherapy, coupled with the strongest risk factors being tied to infrastructure access rather than individual behavior, indicates that structural and environmental conditions are the primary drivers of transmission differences.
Evidence: In Ese-Odo, the reduction in prevalence was modest, decreasing from 39% at baseline to 25.8% at the time of assessment, with prevalence still exceeding 20%.
Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs
The continued transmission of STH in Ese-Odo a riverine LGA is largely attributed to inadequate access to and use of water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities.

Regression models indicated that inadequate hygiene practices, such as failure to use soap after defecation, presented the highest risk for STH infection, followed by the use of unimproved latrines and water sources.
312.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that despite effective PC, Ese-Odo maintains high prevalence due to poor structural conditions like inadequate WASH infrastructure. The strongest risk factors identified are related to access to water and sanitation facilities, not merely individual behaviors, underscoring that structural and environmental conditions drive transmission differences.

Evidence: Their proportion among leucocytes steadily increased from 6 weeks p.e. and peaked at 11 weeks p.e. (53 ± 9% of all immune cells, p = 0.0078 for week 11 (final time tested) vs week 1 p.e.).

suggesting that a balance between both anti- and pro-inflammatory cytokines is needed to achieve disease resolution.

Evidence: both IgG and IgA peaked between 4 and 6 weeks p.e. (p = 0.001 and 0.002 between weeks 0 and 6 for IgG and IgA respectively), and then decreased at 11 weeks p.e. (p = 0.0098 and 0.0039 for IgG and IgA respectively).
the positive correlation with tear IgA was not significant (p = 0.1685).
323.Rationale: Tear IgA did not persist after bacterial clearance; it peaked at weeks 4-6 and decreased significantly by week 11. It also lacked a significant correlation with bacterial load. Thus, it does not meet the criteria of a sustained response associated with disease resolution.

324.Rationale: The passage does not describe a crossover design in which parasites are alternately exposed to drug and no-drug conditions.
